core_concepts: Push Day Architecture: A scientific push workout balances compound movements for mechanical tension (chest, shoulders) and isolation movements for metabolic stress (triceps, lateral delts).
Undulating Loading: Using varied rep ranges within a single session (heavy/low reps to light/high reps) to stimulate different muscle fiber types and manage systemic fatigue.
Stability & Safety: Prioritizing stable platforms (machines) for heavy isolation work while using free weights for compound lifts to maintain stabilizer muscle development.
Tempo Manipulation: Using slow eccentrics (e.g., 5-second negatives) on isolation movements to improve the mind-muscle connection and increase time under tension.
Final Set Intensity: Utilizing AMRAP (As Many Reps As Possible) or failure sets at the end of a session when systemic fatigue from other exercises is no longer a limiting factor.

actionable_advice.sets_and_reps: Compound lifts: 3 sets with varying rep schemes (e.g., 8, 5, 15). Isolation lifts: 3 sets of 10–20 reps.
actionable_advice.intensity: Warm-up pyramid (empty bar -> 50% load -> 75% load) before top sets. Use RPE-based training to ensure quality reps.
actionable_advice.progression_methods: Implement small, incremental load increases. When hitting the top of a rep range, increase weight slightly for the next session.
actionable_advice.recovery_advice: Cap volume at a recoverable level. Utilize machines for movements where stabilizers are fatigued to reduce injury risk in the final stages of a workout.
actionable_advice.exercise_selection: Combine heavy compound presses (incline bench) with targeted deltoid and tricep isolations (machine laterals, skull crushers).

scientific_claims.claim: Machine-based movements allow for training closer to failure with a reduced risk of injury compared to free weights when fatigued.
Increased time under tension (TUT) via slow eccentrics improves neuromuscular control and muscle fiber recruitment.
Varied rep ranges in a single session contribute to comprehensive fiber type stimulation (type I and type II).

coaching_insights: Warm-up pyramids aren't just for injury prevention; they serve as a 'greasing the groove' phase for neuromuscular priming.
AMRAP sets are best placed at the very end of a workout to prevent performance interference with subsequent exercises.
When a trainee loses technical form, the set is over. Machines provide a 'safety net' to extend the set beyond what a free weight could safely allow.
common_mistakes: Ignoring the warm-up pyramid and jumping straight into heavy work.
Losing focus on the eccentric (lowering) phase during the final high-rep sets.
Using momentum on lateral raises instead of isolating the side delt.
Performing too much isolation work before the main compound lift is finished.
Failing to track set-by-set progress (load and reps) in a logbook.

motivational_quotes: Stability is the foundation of strength.
Control the negative, master the growth.
Don't trade your form for weight.
The last rep of the AMRAP is where the work begins.
Prime the nervous system, then build the muscle.
Quality tension beats quantity of weight.
Master the move, then challenge the muscle.
myths_addressed: Myth: You only need to train heavy to grow.
Myth: You should always train to failure on every set.
Myth: You need 20+ exercises per workout.
Myth: Warm-ups are a waste of time.
Myth: Faster lifting tempo builds more explosive muscle.
Myth: You shouldn't do isolation work on compound days.
Myth: The bench press is the only chest exercise that matters.
Myth: Muscle confusion is the key to progress.
Myth: You can't build muscle with high-rep training.

grit_application.goal: Optimize Push Day via tempo control and intensity sequencing.
grit_application.routine: Create a 'Tempo Guide' feature in the app, where specific exercises prompt a 'Slow Eccentric' timer (e.g., 3-5 seconds) for isolation moves.
grit_application.instruction: Implement a 'Warm-up Wizard' that pre-populates a pyramid structure for main compound lifts to ensure neurological priming.
grit_application.tracking: Use an 'Intensity Log' that tags sets as 'Standard' vs. 'AMRAP' to track volume specifically for fatigue management.
